summary:
The Cucuteni-Trypillia culture flourished across the forest-steppe zone of present-day Romania, Moldova, and Ukraine between approximately 5500 and 2750 BCE, representing one of the largest and most enduring Neolithic and Chalcolithic cultures in Europe. Known by its Romanian name (Cucuteni) and Ukrainian name (Trypillia) according to which country's scholarship one follows, the culture is most notable for its distinctive painted pottery, large settlements, and the remarkable phenomenon of the [[event:1582|Trypillia mega-sites]].

Cucuteni-Trypillia communities were farmers cultivating emmer wheat, barley, and lentils on the rich black soils of the Ukrainian steppe fringe. Cattle, pigs, and sheep were herded, and hunting supplemented agricultural production. The culture's painted pottery — decorated with elaborate polychrome geometric spirals, animal forms, and sometimes human figures — ranks among the finest ceramic art of prehistoric Europe and reflects considerable specialized craft knowledge and aesthetic sophistication.

The deliberate burning of settlements, which is a consistent archaeological signature of Cucuteni-Trypillia communities, remains one of the most debated aspects of the culture. Entire villages were systematically torched at regular intervals — perhaps every 60–80 years — with the burned structures then serving as foundations for new constructions on the same site. Whether this practice was ritual, practical, or defensive in nature remains uncertain. The culture eventually declined after 3000 BCE, possibly due to a combination of climate change, soil exhaustion, and pressure from expanding [[event:1538|Pontic-Caspian steppe cultures]].
